criteria: The learner can name the three core features of ADHD and explain how they differ from ordinary distraction.
The learner can describe attention-related and hyperactive or impulsive signs across settings like home, school, and work.
The learner can identify supports that can help, from behavior strategies to school accommodations.
The learner can distinguish useful short-term anxiety from anxiety that overwhelms daily life.
The learner can describe generalized anxiety, panic, and phobias in plain language.
The learner can name common symptoms, risk factors, and the kinds of support that can help.
The learner can describe immediate and long-term benefits of regular physical activity for brain and body.
The learner can state the general weekly activity guideline and adapt it to their own schedule.
The learner can explain how movement relates to mood, sleep, and long-term health risks.
The learner can describe common reactions after a traumatic event and how most ease over time.
The learner can recognize signs that someone may need more support, including how children and teens can differ from adults.
The learner can list healthy coping steps and know when to contact crisis support.
The learner can explain how depression differs from ordinary sadness.
The learner can recognize common symptoms and describe how they can vary by age.
The learner can describe what can help and when to reach out for support.
The learner can describe rehearsal, chunking, elaborative rehearsal, and mnemonic devices.
The learner can apply at least two evidence-based study strategies to real material.
The learner can explain why distributed practice works better than cramming.
The learner can list signs that mental energy is running low.
The learner can choose practical steps that may help them feel better.
The learner can identify when to reach out to a provider or crisis support.
The learner can define mental health as emotional, psychological, and social well-being.
The learner can name several everyday practices that support mental health.
The learner can describe one calming practice and recognize when to seek help.
The learner can define emotion and distinguish it from mood.
The learner can compare the major theories of emotion, from James-Lange to cognitive-mediational.
The learner can name key limbic system structures involved in emotional processing.
The learner can define operant conditioning and the law of effect.
The learner can distinguish the four combinations of reinforcement and punishment.
The learner can explain shaping and why variable ratio schedules are the most resistant to extinction.
The learner can define personality as long-standing patterns of thinking, feeling, and behaving.
The learner can trace how personality theories developed from the four temperaments to modern approaches.
The learner can explain why phrenology was discredited as pseudoscience.
The learner can name several small, repeatable self-care practices.
The learner can match self-care areas such as sleep, movement, and connection to their own routine.
The learner can recognize symptoms that call for more support from a provider.
The learner can describe what sleep does for the brain and body.
The learner can state how much sleep their age group needs and what short sleep costs.
The learner can apply several habits that improve sleep quality.
The learner can define social connection in terms of the number, quality, and variety of relationships.
The learner can explain how supportive relationships relate to health outcomes.
The learner can distinguish emotional support from physical support and name a community source of each.
The learner can describe consciousness as a continuum from full alertness to deep sleep.
The learner can explain how the suprachiasmatic nucleus and melatonin regulate circadian rhythm.
The learner can identify causes and costs of circadian disruption and sleep debt.
The learner can define stress and recognize how it shows up in feelings, body, and behavior.
The learner can name several practices that care for the mind under stress.
The learner can name several practices that care for the body under stress.
The learner can distinguish problem-focused from emotion-focused coping and describe when each fits.
The learner can explain how perceived control and learned helplessness shape responses to stress.
The learner can describe how social support buffers stress and name one stress-reduction technique.
The learner can define cognition and describe what cognitive psychology studies.
The learner can distinguish natural from artificial concepts and give an example of a prototype.
The learner can explain how schemata speed up thinking and where they can mislead.
